metadata.title: Google Associate Cloud Engineer ACE Practice Questions

1.name: Setting Up a Cloud Solution Environment

2.name: Planning and Configuring a Cloud Solution

3.name: Deploying and Implementing a Cloud Solution

questions.question: What is the highest level of the Google Cloud resource hierarchy?
options: Organization node
Folder
Project
Billing account
questions.explanation: The Organization node is the root of the Google Cloud resource hierarchy. It represents your company and sits above all folders and projects. It is automatically created when you sign up with Google Workspace or Cloud Identity.

questions.question: Which resource in the Google Cloud hierarchy directly contains resources like Compute Engine instances and Cloud Storage buckets?
options: Project
Organization
Folder
Resource group
questions.explanation: Projects are the base-level organizing entity in Google Cloud. All resources such as VMs, storage buckets, and databases belong to a project. Projects provide isolation, billing boundaries, and API management.

questions.question: What is the purpose of folders in the Google Cloud resource hierarchy?
options: To group projects and apply policies at an intermediate level
To store files and documents for teams
To define billing boundaries for cost allocation
To create virtual networks between projects
questions.explanation: Folders provide an additional grouping mechanism between the Organization node and projects. They can be used to model departments, teams, or environments (dev/staging/prod), and IAM policies applied to a folder are inherited by all projects within it.

questions.question: Your company wants to separate development, staging, and production environments while applying different IAM policies to each. What is the recommended approach?
options: Create separate folders for each environment and assign IAM policies at the folder level
Create a single project and use labels to differentiate environments
Create separate billing accounts for each environment
Create separate organizations for each environment
questions.explanation: Using folders for environment separation allows you to apply IAM policies that are inherited by all projects within each folder. This provides clean isolation while maintaining a single organizational structure. Separate organizations would create unnecessary management overhead.

questions.question: Which command creates a new Google Cloud project using the gcloud CLI?
options: gcloud projects create PROJECT_ID
gcloud create project PROJECT_ID
gcloud new project PROJECT_ID
gcloud init project PROJECT_ID
questions.explanation: The command 'gcloud projects create PROJECT_ID' creates a new Google Cloud project. You can optionally specify --name for a display name and --folder or --organization for placement in the resource hierarchy.

questions.question: You need to link a billing account to a project so the team can start provisioning resources. Which command accomplishes this?
options: gcloud billing projects link PROJECT_ID --billing-account=ACCOUNT_ID
gcloud projects set-billing PROJECT_ID --account=ACCOUNT_ID
gcloud billing link PROJECT_ID ACCOUNT_ID
gcloud config set billing/account ACCOUNT_ID
questions.explanation: The command 'gcloud billing projects link' links a project to a billing account. A project must be linked to a billing account before any billable resources can be created within it.

questions.question: A project has exceeded its budget and you need to prevent further charges while keeping the project resources for review. What should you do?
options: Disable the billing account linked to the project
Delete the project immediately
Set a budget alert at 100%
Remove all IAM bindings from the project
questions.explanation: Disabling billing on a project stops all charges while preserving the resources for review. Budget alerts only send notifications but do not stop charges. Deleting the project would destroy all resources. Removing IAM bindings would not stop automated processes from incurring charges.

questions.question: Your organization wants to set up billing so that different departments are charged for their own cloud usage, but a central team manages overall spending. What is the best approach?
options: Use a single billing account with separate projects per department and set up billing export to BigQuery for cost allocation
Create separate billing accounts for each department
Use a single project with labels for each department
Create separate organizations for each department
questions.explanation: Using a single billing account with separate projects per department provides centralized cost management while allowing per-department tracking. Billing export to BigQuery enables detailed cost analysis and allocation. Separate billing accounts would reduce centralized visibility.

questions.question: Which command installs the Google Cloud CLI on a local machine?
options: Run the install script from the downloaded Cloud SDK archive
pip install gcloud
npm install -g @google-cloud/cli
apt-get install gcloud
questions.explanation: The Google Cloud CLI is installed by downloading the SDK archive and running the install script (install.sh or install.bat). While some Linux distributions offer packages via apt-get for google-cloud-cli, the standard method is to download and run the install script from cloud.google.com.

questions.question: Which command initializes the gcloud CLI and sets the default project, region, and zone?
options: gcloud init
gcloud setup
gcloud configure
gcloud start
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud init' command initializes the CLI, authenticates with your Google account, and sets default configurations including project, region, and zone. It runs an interactive setup wizard for first-time configuration.

questions.question: You want to manage multiple sets of gcloud CLI configurations for different projects and accounts. Which command creates a new named configuration?
options: gcloud config configurations create CONFIG_NAME
gcloud config create CONFIG_NAME
gcloud profiles create CONFIG_NAME
gcloud init --configuration=CONFIG_NAME
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud config configurations create' command creates a new named configuration. You can switch between configurations using 'gcloud config configurations activate CONFIG_NAME'. This is useful for managing multiple projects or accounts.

questions.question: A developer needs to use the gcloud CLI in a CI/CD pipeline where interactive login is not possible. Which authentication method should be used?
options: Activate a service account using gcloud auth activate-service-account with a key file
Run gcloud auth login and copy the tokens
Use gcloud init in the pipeline
Set the GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT environment variable
questions.explanation: For non-interactive environments like CI/CD pipelines, you should use service account authentication via 'gcloud auth activate-service-account --key-file=KEY_FILE.json'. This allows headless authentication without user interaction. Alternatively, workload identity federation can be used for keyless authentication.

questions.question: What must be done before you can use a Google Cloud API in a project?
options: Enable the API in the project
Create a service account for the API
Set up a VPN connection
Configure a firewall rule
questions.explanation: Before using any Google Cloud API, you must enable it in your project. This can be done via the Console, or using 'gcloud services enable SERVICE_NAME'. APIs are disabled by default in new projects to prevent unintended usage and billing.

questions.question: Which command enables the Compute Engine API in the current project?
options: gcloud services enable compute.googleapis.com
gcloud apis enable compute
gcloud compute enable-api
gcloud enable compute.googleapis.com
questions.explanation: The command 'gcloud services enable compute.googleapis.com' enables the Compute Engine API. All Google Cloud APIs follow the format 'service.googleapis.com' and must be enabled before use.

questions.question: You want to restrict which APIs can be enabled in projects under a specific folder. Which approach should you use?
options: Use an Organization Policy with the serviceuser.services constraint
Remove the Service Usage Admin role from all users
Disable all APIs at the organization level
Create a custom IAM role that excludes API enablement
questions.explanation: Organization Policy constraints like 'serviceuser.services' (or 'constraints/serviceuser.services') allow you to define an allowlist or denylist of APIs that can be enabled in projects. This is applied at the folder or organization level and inherited by child resources.

questions.question: What is Cloud Shell in Google Cloud?
options: A browser-based terminal with a pre-configured environment and gcloud CLI pre-installed
A managed virtual machine for running production workloads
A mobile application for managing Google Cloud resources
A desktop IDE for writing Cloud Functions
questions.explanation: Cloud Shell provides a free, browser-based shell environment with gcloud CLI, kubectl, and other tools pre-installed. It includes a 5 GB persistent home directory and runs on a temporary Compute Engine VM.

questions.question: You need to create a budget alert that sends notifications to a Pub/Sub topic when spending reaches 50%, 90%, and 100% of the budget. Which steps are required?
options: Create a budget in Cloud Billing, set threshold rules at 50%, 90%, and 100%, and connect a Pub/Sub topic for programmatic notifications
Create a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy based on billing metrics
Set up a Cloud Scheduler job to check billing data hourly
Write a Cloud Function that queries the Billing API daily
questions.explanation: Cloud Billing budgets support threshold-based alerts that can notify via email and Pub/Sub. Connecting a Pub/Sub topic allows programmatic responses to budget alerts, such as disabling billing or shutting down resources via Cloud Functions triggered by Pub/Sub messages.

questions.question: Which command sets the default project for all subsequent gcloud commands?
options: gcloud config set project PROJECT_ID
gcloud set default-project PROJECT_ID
gcloud project set-default PROJECT_ID
gcloud default project PROJECT_ID
questions.explanation: The command 'gcloud config set project PROJECT_ID' sets the default project for the active CLI configuration. All subsequent gcloud commands will use this project unless overridden with the --project flag.

questions.question: Which command lists all APIs that are currently enabled in the active project?
options: gcloud services list --enabled
gcloud apis list --active
gcloud services show-enabled
gcloud project list-apis
questions.explanation: The command 'gcloud services list --enabled' displays all APIs currently enabled in the active project. You can also use '--available' to see all available APIs or '--filter' to search for specific services.

questions.question: A company is migrating to Google Cloud and needs to establish a resource hierarchy. They have 3 business units, each with dev and prod environments. What is the recommended hierarchy structure?
options: Organization > Business Unit Folders > Environment Folders (dev/prod) > Projects
Organization > Environment Folders (dev/prod) > Business Unit Projects
Organization > Single Folder > All Projects with labels
Three Organizations > Environment Projects per Organization
questions.explanation: The recommended approach uses nested folders: Business Unit folders under the Organization, with Environment folders (dev/prod) nested within each. This allows IAM policies to be applied at the business unit level and environment-specific policies at the nested folder level, providing granular control.

questions.question: How can you export Cloud Billing data for detailed analysis and reporting?
options: Set up billing export to BigQuery
Download invoices from the Billing Console as CSV
Use Cloud Monitoring to view billing metrics
Enable the Cloud Billing API and call it hourly
questions.explanation: Cloud Billing export to BigQuery provides detailed, queryable billing data including cost, usage, and pricing information. This allows for custom reports, cost allocation dashboards, and trend analysis using SQL queries.

questions.question: You need to set the default region and zone for a gcloud configuration without running the interactive setup. Which commands should you use?
options: gcloud config set compute/region REGION and gcloud config set compute/zone ZONE
gcloud init --region=REGION --zone=ZONE
gcloud config set default-region REGION
gcloud compute set-defaults --region=REGION --zone=ZONE
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud config set compute/region' and 'gcloud config set compute/zone' commands set defaults for Compute Engine operations. These values are used by gcloud commands when --region or --zone flags are not explicitly specified.

questions.question: What happens to resources when a Google Cloud project is deleted?
options: The project enters a 30-day pending deletion state, after which all resources are permanently deleted
All resources are immediately deleted with no recovery option
Resources are moved to the organization's default project
Resources are archived and can be restored at any time
questions.explanation: When a project is deleted, it enters a 30-day pending deletion state during which it can be recovered. After the 30-day period, the project and all associated resources are permanently deleted and cannot be recovered.

questions.question: You need to enable multiple APIs simultaneously in a project. Which command is most efficient?
options: gcloud services enable compute.googleapis.com storage.googleapis.com bigquery.googleapis.com
gcloud services enable --all
gcloud services batch-enable compute storage bigquery
You must enable each API separately in individual commands
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud services enable' command accepts multiple API names in a single command, separated by spaces. This is more efficient than running separate commands for each API and reduces the number of API calls needed.

questions.question: What is the minimum requirement before you can create billable resources in a Google Cloud project?
options: The project must be linked to a billing account
A budget must be set for the project
An organization node must exist
A service account must be created
questions.explanation: A project must be linked to an active billing account before any billable resources can be created. Without a billing account, only free-tier resources and services that do not incur charges can be used.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud resource provides a domain-wide identity for your organization?
options: Cloud Identity
Cloud IAM
Resource Manager
Access Context Manager
questions.explanation: Cloud Identity provides centralized identity management and is the foundation for the Organization resource in Google Cloud. It allows you to manage users and groups that can be used for IAM policy bindings across the entire organization.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'gcloud auth login' and 'gcloud auth application-default login'?
options: 'gcloud auth login' authenticates the user for gcloud CLI commands, while 'gcloud auth application-default login' sets credentials for application code using client libraries
They are identical commands with different aliases
'gcloud auth login' is for service accounts while 'application-default login' is for user accounts
'gcloud auth application-default login' authenticates for the Console UI
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud auth login' authenticates the user for gcloud CLI tool operations. The 'gcloud auth application-default login' creates Application Default Credentials (ADC) that are used by client libraries and tools that follow the ADC flow for local development.

questions.question: Your organization wants to prevent any team from accidentally incurring costs on a specific internal project. Budget alerts alone are not sufficient. What should you do?
options: Set up a budget with Pub/Sub notifications that trigger a Cloud Function to disable billing on the project when the threshold is reached
Set a budget alert at 100% and trust teams to respond promptly
Remove all IAM roles from the project
Delete the billing account associated with the project
questions.explanation: To automatically cap spending, configure a budget with a Pub/Sub notification and a Cloud Function that disables billing when the threshold is reached. Budget alerts alone are passive notifications that require human action. Deleting the billing account would affect other projects linked to it.

questions.question: You accidentally disabled the Compute Engine API in a project that has running VMs. What happens to the existing VMs?
options: The VMs continue to run, but you cannot manage them through the API or Console until the API is re-enabled
All VMs are immediately terminated
The VMs are automatically migrated to another project
Nothing changes; the API cannot be disabled while resources exist
questions.explanation: Disabling an API does not immediately destroy resources that already exist. However, you will not be able to manage, modify, or interact with those resources through the API, Console, or gcloud CLI until the API is re-enabled. Running VMs continue to operate.

questions.question: Your company is acquiring another company that already uses Google Cloud. How should you integrate their resources into your existing organization?
options: Migrate their projects into your organization using the Resource Manager API and update IAM policies accordingly
Create a new organization and share resources between organizations using VPC peering
Delete their organization and recreate all resources in your organization
There is no way to move projects between organizations
questions.explanation: Projects can be migrated between organizations using the Resource Manager API. This requires appropriate permissions in both organizations. After migration, IAM policies should be updated to align with the new organizational structure. This preserves existing resources and configurations.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service provides virtual machines?
options: Compute Engine
App Engine
Cloud Functions
Cloud Run
questions.explanation: Compute Engine is Google Cloud's IaaS offering that provides virtual machines. It allows full control over the OS, networking, and storage configuration of VMs, and supports custom machine types, preemptible VMs, and various OS images.

questions.question: Which compute service is best suited for running containerized applications with automatic scaling and no cluster management?
options: Cloud Run
Compute Engine
Google Kubernetes Engine
App Engine Standard
questions.explanation: Cloud Run is a fully managed serverless platform for running containers. It automatically scales to zero when not in use and scales up based on traffic, without requiring cluster management. It supports any language or framework that can be containerized.

questions.question: A team needs to run a stateless web application that requires a specific runtime environment with custom system libraries. Which service is most appropriate?
options: App Engine Flexible Environment
App Engine Standard Environment
Cloud Functions
Cloud Scheduler
questions.explanation: App Engine Flexible Environment runs application containers on Compute Engine VMs, supporting custom runtimes and system libraries. Standard Environment is limited to specific language runtimes. Cloud Functions is for event-driven functions, not full web applications with custom libraries.

questions.question: What is the key difference between GKE Standard mode and GKE Autopilot mode?
options: Autopilot manages the nodes automatically and charges per pod, while Standard mode requires manual node management and charges per node
Standard mode is free tier while Autopilot is paid only
Autopilot does not support persistent volumes
Standard mode cannot auto-scale while Autopilot can
questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot mode is a fully managed Kubernetes experience where Google manages the nodes, scaling, and security. You pay per pod resource request. In Standard mode, you manage node pools, node sizing, and pay for the nodes regardless of pod utilization.

questions.question: A startup needs to run a computation-heavy batch job once daily. Cost is the top priority. Which compute option is most cost-effective?
options: Compute Engine with Spot VMs
Cloud Run with always-on minimum instances
GKE Autopilot with guaranteed pods
App Engine Standard with manual scaling
questions.explanation: Spot VMs (formerly preemptible VMs) offer up to 60-91% discount compared to regular VMs. For batch jobs that can tolerate interruptions, Spot VMs provide the most cost-effective compute option. The daily batch can be designed to checkpoint progress and resume if preempted.

questions.question: Which Cloud Storage class is best for data that is accessed less than once a month?
options: Coldline Storage
Standard Storage
Nearline Storage
Archive Storage
questions.explanation: Coldline Storage is designed for data accessed less than once a quarter (roughly once a month or less). It has lower storage costs than Standard or Nearline but higher retrieval costs. For data accessed less than once a year, Archive Storage is more appropriate.

questions.question: Your application needs a relational database with strong consistency and automatic scaling to handle traffic spikes. It must support multi-region deployments. Which service should you choose?
options: Cloud Spanner
Cloud SQL
Cloud Bigtable
Firestore
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner is a fully managed, globally distributed relational database that provides strong consistency, horizontal scaling, and multi-region deployments. Cloud SQL supports regional deployments but does not natively support horizontal scaling or multi-region writes.

questions.question: Which storage solution is recommended for storing structured data from IoT devices that requires low-latency, high-throughput read/write access?
options: Cloud Bigtable
Cloud SQL
Cloud Storage
BigQuery
questions.explanation: Cloud Bigtable is a NoSQL wide-column database designed for high-throughput, low-latency workloads. It is ideal for IoT time-series data, financial data, and operational analytics. It can handle millions of operations per second with consistent sub-10ms latency.

questions.question: A company stores 500 TB of log data in Cloud Storage for compliance. The data must be retained for 7 years but is only accessed during annual audits. What is the most cost-effective storage configuration?
options: Archive Storage with a retention policy of 7 years
Standard Storage with lifecycle rules to delete after 7 years
Coldline Storage with object versioning enabled
Nearline Storage with a bucket lock
questions.explanation: Archive Storage has the lowest storage cost and is designed for data accessed less than once a year. A retention policy ensures the data cannot be deleted before the 7-year requirement. This is the most cost-effective option for rarely accessed compliance data.

questions.question: What is a VPC in Google Cloud?
options: A Virtual Private Cloud that provides networking for cloud resources
A virtual machine pricing calculator
A container orchestration platform
A managed database service
questions.explanation: A VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) is a virtual network that provides networking functionality for Google Cloud resources. VPCs are global resources that contain regional subnets, firewall rules, and routes. Resources like Compute Engine VMs and GKE clusters are deployed into VPC subnets.

questions.question: What is the difference between auto mode and custom mode VPC networks?
options: Auto mode automatically creates one subnet in each region, while custom mode requires you to create subnets manually
Auto mode is free while custom mode incurs charges
Custom mode networks cannot span multiple regions
Auto mode supports IPv6 while custom mode does not
questions.explanation: Auto mode VPCs automatically create one subnet in each region with predefined IP ranges. Custom mode VPCs give you full control over subnet creation, IP ranges, and regional placement. Custom mode is recommended for production use as it avoids IP range conflicts.

questions.question: Which network feature allows two VPC networks to communicate internally without traffic traversing the public internet?
options: VPC Network Peering
Cloud CDN
Cloud Endpoints
Traffic Director
questions.explanation: VPC Network Peering allows two VPC networks to connect and communicate using internal IP addresses. Traffic between peered networks stays within Google's network and does not traverse the public internet. Peering works across projects and organizations.

questions.question: Your company has offices in New York and London. You need a network design where on-premises resources in both locations can communicate with Google Cloud workloads with high bandwidth and low latency. What should you use?
options: Dedicated Interconnect connections in both locations
Cloud VPN with dynamic routing
VPC Network Peering
Cloud CDN with custom origins
questions.explanation: Dedicated Interconnect provides high-bandwidth, low-latency connectivity between on-premises networks and Google Cloud VPCs. For two office locations, you would set up Dedicated Interconnect in colocation facilities near each office. This provides more consistent performance than VPN for enterprise workloads.

questions.question: Which Cloud Functions trigger fires when a file is uploaded to Cloud Storage?
options: Cloud Storage trigger (google.cloud.storage.object.v1.finalized)
HTTP trigger
Pub/Sub trigger
Firestore trigger
questions.explanation: Cloud Functions supports Cloud Storage event triggers that fire on object changes. The 'finalized' event triggers when a new object is created or an existing object is overwritten. Other storage events include 'deleted', 'archived', and 'metadataUpdated'.

questions.question: An e-commerce application needs a database for its product catalog (SQL queries), user sessions (low-latency key-value), and analytics (complex aggregations over terabytes). Which combination of services should you use?
options: Cloud SQL for the product catalog, Memorystore for user sessions, and BigQuery for analytics
Cloud Spanner for all three use cases
Firestore for all three use cases
Cloud Bigtable for all three use cases
questions.explanation: Using purpose-built services for each workload is the recommended approach. Cloud SQL handles relational product catalog queries, Memorystore (Redis) provides sub-millisecond latency for session data, and BigQuery handles large-scale analytics. Cloud Spanner could handle catalog and sessions but would be costly for analytics.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service provides a managed load balancer that can distribute traffic across multiple regions?
options: Global External Application Load Balancer
Cloud NAT
Cloud DNS
Cloud Interconnect
questions.explanation: The Global External Application Load Balancer (formerly HTTP(S) Load Balancer) distributes traffic across multiple regions using a single anycast IP. It supports URL-based routing, SSL termination, and integration with Cloud CDN and Cloud Armor.

questions.question: Which machine type family should you choose for memory-intensive workloads like in-memory databases?
options: M-series (memory-optimized)
N-series (general-purpose)
C-series (compute-optimized)
A-series (accelerator-optimized)
questions.explanation: M-series machine types (m2, m3) are memory-optimized and offer the highest memory-to-CPU ratios. They support up to 12 TB of memory and are designed for in-memory databases like SAP HANA, large caches, and memory-intensive analytics.

questions.question: What is the minimum storage duration for Nearline Storage in Cloud Storage?
options: 30 days
90 days
365 days
No minimum
questions.explanation: Nearline Storage has a minimum storage duration of 30 days. If an object is deleted or replaced before 30 days, you are still charged for 30 days of storage. Nearline is designed for data accessed less than once a month.

questions.question: You are designing a network for a multi-tier application. The web tier should be accessible from the internet, but the database tier must only be accessible from the application tier. What is the best approach?
options: Place the web tier in a public subnet with external IPs and the database tier in a private subnet with firewall rules allowing traffic only from the application tier
Place all tiers in the same subnet and use application-level authentication
Use separate VPC networks for each tier with VPC Peering
Use Cloud NAT for all tiers to hide IP addresses
questions.explanation: Using separate subnets with firewall rules provides network-level isolation. The web tier gets external IPs for public access, while the database tier sits in a private subnet. Firewall rules restrict database access to only the application tier's IP range. This follows the principle of least privilege at the network layer.

questions.question: A company wants to migrate a legacy monolithic application to Google Cloud. The application requires Windows Server, has heavy CPU usage, and cannot be containerized. Which service is most appropriate?
options: Compute Engine with a Windows Server image
Cloud Run
App Engine Standard
GKE with Windows node pools
questions.explanation: Compute Engine supports Windows Server VMs with various machine types. For a monolithic application that cannot be containerized and requires Windows, Compute Engine provides the most straightforward lift-and-shift migration path with full OS control.

questions.question: Which Persistent Disk type provides the highest IOPS for a Compute Engine instance?
options: pd-extreme
pd-ssd
pd-balanced
pd-standard
questions.explanation: pd-extreme provides the highest IOPS (up to 120,000 IOPS) and is designed for the most demanding workloads. pd-ssd provides up to 100,000 IOPS, pd-balanced up to 80,000, and pd-standard up to 7,500. pd-extreme is available on selected machine types.

questions.question: Which App Engine environment supports automatic scaling to zero instances when there is no traffic?
options: Standard Environment
Flexible Environment
Both environments support scaling to zero
Neither environment supports scaling to zero
questions.explanation: App Engine Standard Environment supports scaling to zero instances when there is no traffic, meaning you pay nothing when idle. Flexible Environment runs on Compute Engine VMs and maintains at least one instance, so it does not scale to zero.

questions.question: What is Cloud DNS used for in Google Cloud?
options: Managing domain name resolution and DNS records
Encrypting network traffic between regions
Load balancing HTTP requests
Monitoring network performance
questions.explanation: Cloud DNS is a high-performance, resilient, global DNS service that publishes domain names to the global DNS. It supports public and private DNS zones, DNSSEC, and DNS policies for controlling name resolution within VPC networks.

questions.question: Your application stores user-uploaded images in Cloud Storage. You need to automatically resize images when they are uploaded. What is the most cost-effective serverless approach?
options: Create a Cloud Function triggered by Cloud Storage object finalization that resizes the image and stores the result
Run a Compute Engine VM that polls the bucket every minute for new files
Use a GKE cluster with a CronJob to check for new images
Deploy an App Engine Flexible app that monitors the bucket
questions.explanation: A Cloud Function triggered by Cloud Storage events is the most cost-effective approach for this use case. It runs only when an image is uploaded (pay per invocation), requires no infrastructure management, and automatically scales with upload volume.

questions.question: Which type of Cloud VPN supports dynamic routing using BGP?
options: HA VPN
Classic VPN with static routing
Cloud NAT
Partner Interconnect
questions.explanation: HA VPN supports dynamic routing via BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) through Cloud Router. This allows automatic route advertisement between your on-premises network and Google Cloud VPC. HA VPN provides 99.99% availability SLA with two tunnels in an active/active configuration.

questions.question: What is the advantage of using a custom machine type in Compute Engine?
options: You can specify the exact number of vCPUs and amount of memory needed, optimizing cost for your workload
Custom machine types are always cheaper than predefined types
Custom machine types have higher performance limits
Custom machine types include free storage
questions.explanation: Custom machine types allow you to specify exact vCPU and memory configurations. This is useful when predefined machine types don't match your workload needs, as you pay only for the resources you specify rather than paying for over-provisioned predefined types.

questions.question: Which Cloud Storage feature automatically transitions objects between storage classes based on age or access patterns?
options: Object Lifecycle Management
Object Versioning
Retention Policies
Bucket Lock
questions.explanation: Object Lifecycle Management allows you to define rules that automatically transition objects to less expensive storage classes or delete them based on conditions like age, creation date, or number of newer versions. This helps optimize storage costs without manual intervention.

questions.question: You need to connect your on-premises data center to Google Cloud with a bandwidth of 100 Gbps. Which connectivity option should you choose?
options: Dedicated Interconnect with multiple 10 Gbps or 100 Gbps VLAN attachments
HA VPN with multiple tunnels
Partner Interconnect
Cloud CDN with custom origins
questions.explanation: Dedicated Interconnect supports 10 Gbps or 100 Gbps connections between your on-premises network and Google Cloud. For 100 Gbps total bandwidth, you can use a single 100 Gbps connection or multiple VLAN attachments. This requires a colocation facility where Google is present.

questions.question: Which compute service allows you to run event-driven code without managing servers?
options: Cloud Functions
Compute Engine
Google Kubernetes Engine
Bare Metal Solution
questions.explanation: Cloud Functions is a serverless, event-driven compute service. You write individual functions that are triggered by events (HTTP requests, Pub/Sub messages, Cloud Storage changes). Google manages all infrastructure, and you pay only for the execution time.

questions.question: Which database service is a fully managed MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server offering?
options: Cloud SQL
Cloud Spanner
BigQuery
Firestore
questions.explanation: Cloud SQL is a fully managed relational database service that supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server. It handles backups, replication, patches, and updates while providing high availability and automatic storage increases.

questions.question: Which command creates a new Compute Engine instance?
options: gcloud compute instances create INSTANCE_NAME
gcloud create instance INSTANCE_NAME
gcloud vm create INSTANCE_NAME
gcloud compute new-instance INSTANCE_NAME
questions.explanation: The command 'gcloud compute instances create INSTANCE_NAME' creates a new Compute Engine VM. You can specify machine type, zone, image, disk, network, and other options as flags.

questions.question: What is a startup script in Compute Engine?
options: A script that runs automatically when a VM instance starts up, used to install software or configure the instance
A script that creates the VM instance
A script that runs during the billing setup process
A script that monitors VM performance
questions.explanation: A startup script runs each time a Compute Engine instance boots. It is commonly used to install packages, configure services, or pull application code. Startup scripts can be specified inline or stored in Cloud Storage.

questions.question: You need to create multiple identical VMs for a web server fleet. What is the recommended approach?
options: Create an instance template and use it with a managed instance group
Manually create each VM with the same configuration
Create one VM and clone it multiple times
Use Cloud Shell to run the create command in a loop
questions.explanation: Instance templates define VM configurations (machine type, disk, network, startup script) and are used with managed instance groups (MIGs) for creating identical VMs. MIGs provide auto-healing, auto-scaling, and rolling updates for the VM fleet.

questions.question: How do you create a custom image from an existing Compute Engine VM?
options: Stop the VM, then create an image from its boot disk using gcloud compute images create
Export the VM to Cloud Storage as an OVA file
Take a screenshot of the VM configuration and recreate it
Use gcloud compute instances export to create the image
questions.explanation: To create a custom image, you first stop the VM (recommended for consistency), then use 'gcloud compute images create IMAGE_NAME --source-disk=DISK_NAME --source-disk-zone=ZONE'. The image can then be used to create new identical VMs.

questions.question: You need to deploy a Compute Engine VM with a GPU for machine learning training. Which additional configuration is required?
options: Select a zone that supports GPUs, attach a GPU to the VM, and install NVIDIA drivers
Simply select a GPU machine type; drivers are pre-installed
GPUs are automatically assigned to all VMs in GPU-enabled regions
Create a separate GPU project and link it to the VM
questions.explanation: GPU availability varies by zone. You must choose a zone that supports your desired GPU type, explicitly attach the GPU to the VM configuration, and install appropriate NVIDIA drivers (or use a GPU-optimized image with drivers pre-installed like the Deep Learning VM image).

questions.question: Which command creates a GKE cluster?
options: gcloud container clusters create CLUSTER_NAME
gcloud gke create CLUSTER_NAME
kubectl create cluster CLUSTER_NAME
gcloud kubernetes create CLUSTER_NAME
questions.explanation: The command 'gcloud container clusters create CLUSTER_NAME' creates a new GKE cluster. You can specify zone, machine type, number of nodes, and many other options. After creation, use 'gcloud container clusters get-credentials' to configure kubectl.

questions.question: After creating a GKE cluster, what must you do before running kubectl commands against it?
options: Run gcloud container clusters get-credentials to configure kubectl
Install a separate kubectl plugin for GKE
Manually edit the kubeconfig file with cluster details
Restart the Cloud Shell terminal
questions.explanation: Running 'gcloud container clusters get-credentials CLUSTER_NAME' configures kubectl with the cluster endpoint and authentication credentials. This updates the kubeconfig file (~/.kube/config) so kubectl commands are directed to the correct cluster.

questions.question: You need to deploy a containerized application on GKE and expose it to the internet. Which Kubernetes resources do you need?
options: A Deployment (to manage pods) and a Service of type LoadBalancer (to expose it externally)
Only a Pod definition
A DaemonSet and a ClusterIP Service
A StatefulSet and a NodePort Service
questions.explanation: A Deployment manages the desired state of your pods (replicas, updates, rollbacks). A Service of type LoadBalancer creates a Google Cloud load balancer that routes external traffic to the pods. Together they provide a production-ready, externally accessible application.

questions.question: A team needs to run a database in GKE that requires stable network identifiers and persistent storage per pod. Which workload type should they use?
options: StatefulSet with PersistentVolumeClaims
Deployment with emptyDir volumes
DaemonSet with hostPath volumes
Job with configMap volumes
questions.explanation: StatefulSets provide stable, unique network identifiers, stable persistent storage, and ordered deployment and scaling. This is essential for databases that require consistent identity and persistent data. PersistentVolumeClaims ensure each pod has its own dedicated storage.

questions.question: Which command deploys an application to App Engine?
options: gcloud app deploy
gcloud deploy app
gcloud appengine deploy
gcloud services deploy app
questions.explanation: The command 'gcloud app deploy' deploys the application defined in the app.yaml file to App Engine. It packages the application, uploads it, and creates a new version. You can also specify a specific YAML file as an argument.

questions.question: You deployed a new version of your App Engine application and it has a critical bug. How do you quickly roll back to the previous version?
options: Use traffic splitting to route 100% of traffic to the previous version
Delete the current version and redeploy the old code
Restore from a backup
There is no way to roll back in App Engine
questions.explanation: App Engine maintains previous versions. You can use traffic splitting or 'gcloud app services set-traffic --splits=VERSION=1' to route all traffic to a previous working version. This provides instant rollback without redeploying code.

questions.question: You want to gradually migrate traffic from an old App Engine version to a new one. What is the recommended approach?
options: Use traffic splitting to send a percentage of traffic to the new version, gradually increasing it as confidence grows
Deploy the new version and immediately switch all traffic
Run both versions on different URLs and ask users to switch manually
Use Cloud DNS to route traffic between versions
questions.explanation: App Engine traffic splitting allows you to specify percentages for different versions (e.g., 90% old, 10% new). You can use IP-based or cookie-based splitting. Gradually increasing traffic to the new version minimizes risk and allows you to catch issues before full migration.

questions.question: Which command deploys a Cloud Function triggered by HTTP?
options: gcloud functions deploy FUNCTION_NAME --trigger-http --runtime=python311
gcloud deploy function FUNCTION_NAME --http
gcloud serverless deploy FUNCTION_NAME
gcloud cloud-functions create FUNCTION_NAME
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud functions deploy' command deploys a Cloud Function. The --trigger-http flag creates an HTTP endpoint, and --runtime specifies the language runtime. You can also set --memory, --timeout, and other configuration options.

questions.question: What is the maximum execution timeout for Cloud Functions (2nd gen)?
options: 60 minutes
9 minutes
15 minutes
5 minutes
questions.explanation: Cloud Functions 2nd gen supports a maximum timeout of 60 minutes, compared to 9 minutes for 1st gen. 2nd gen is built on Cloud Run and offers larger instance sizes, concurrency, and traffic splitting in addition to longer timeouts.

questions.question: Which command deploys a container image to Cloud Run?
options: gcloud run deploy SERVICE_NAME --image=IMAGE_URL
gcloud cloudrun create SERVICE_NAME --container=IMAGE_URL
gcloud deploy run SERVICE_NAME --image=IMAGE_URL
gcloud containers run SERVICE_NAME --image=IMAGE_URL
questions.explanation: The command 'gcloud run deploy SERVICE_NAME --image=IMAGE_URL' deploys a container image to Cloud Run. You can specify the region, allow unauthenticated access, set memory limits, and configure other options.

questions.question: What is the key difference between Cloud Run and Cloud Functions?
options: Cloud Run runs any container image while Cloud Functions runs individual functions in supported runtimes
Cloud Run is more expensive than Cloud Functions
Cloud Functions supports more languages than Cloud Run
Cloud Run cannot scale to zero
questions.explanation: Cloud Run accepts any container image (any language, any framework) while Cloud Functions executes individual functions in specific supported runtimes. Cloud Run is ideal when you need more control over the runtime environment. Both scale to zero and are serverless.

questions.question: You need to deploy a Cloud Run service that connects to a Cloud SQL database in a private VPC. What configuration is required?
options: Configure a VPC connector (Serverless VPC Access) for the Cloud Run service to route traffic through the VPC
Cloud Run can directly connect to Cloud SQL private IP without any additional configuration
Deploy Cloud SQL with a public IP and whitelist Cloud Run's IP range
Use Cloud Interconnect to connect Cloud Run to the VPC
questions.explanation: Cloud Run services run outside your VPC by default. To connect to resources with private IPs (like Cloud SQL), you need a Serverless VPC Access connector. This creates a bridge between the serverless environment and your VPC, allowing Cloud Run to reach private resources.

questions.question: You need to load a large CSV dataset into BigQuery. Which method is most appropriate for a one-time import?
options: Use bq load to import the CSV file directly from Cloud Storage or a local file
Write a Dataflow pipeline to read the CSV and insert rows one at a time
Use the BigQuery streaming insert API
Create the table manually and insert rows via SQL INSERT statements
questions.explanation: The 'bq load' command is the simplest way to import data into BigQuery. It supports CSV, JSON, Avro, Parquet, and ORC files from Cloud Storage or local files. For one-time imports, batch loading is more cost-effective than streaming inserts.

questions.question: You need to deploy a Cloud SQL instance with high availability for a production application. Which configuration provides automatic failover?
options: Create a Cloud SQL instance with high availability enabled, which provisions a standby instance in a different zone
Create two Cloud SQL instances and use application-level failover logic
Enable Cloud SQL read replicas in the same zone
Use Cloud SQL with a persistent disk snapshot schedule
questions.explanation: Enabling high availability on Cloud SQL creates a standby instance in a different zone within the same region. In case of a failure, Cloud SQL automatically fails over to the standby with minimal downtime. This is different from read replicas, which are for read scaling, not failover.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a managed instance group (MIG) in Compute Engine?
options: To manage a group of identical VM instances with auto-scaling, auto-healing, and rolling updates
To group different types of VMs for billing purposes
To create a network between VMs in different regions
To share storage between multiple VM instances
questions.explanation: A managed instance group (MIG) creates and manages a group of identical VM instances based on an instance template. MIGs provide auto-scaling (based on metrics), auto-healing (replacing unhealthy VMs), rolling updates, and load balancer integration.

questions.question: You need to deploy a GKE cluster that provides the highest level of node security with minimal management overhead. Which configuration should you choose?
options: GKE Autopilot with Shielded GKE Nodes enabled
GKE Standard with manual node management
GKE Standard with preemptible nodes
GKE on bare metal
questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot mode provides the highest level of managed security. It enforces security best practices by default: Shielded GKE Nodes, Workload Identity, secure boot, and node auto-management. Google handles node security patching and configuration, reducing management overhead.

questions.question: A Cloud Function needs to access a secret stored in Secret Manager. How should you configure access?
options: Grant the Cloud Function's service account the Secret Manager Secret Accessor role and reference the secret in the function configuration
Hardcode the secret value in the function source code
Store the secret as an environment variable in the function's deployment command
Create a separate API key for the Cloud Function
questions.explanation: Cloud Functions can reference Secret Manager secrets directly in their configuration. The function's service account needs the 'roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor' role. Secrets can be mounted as environment variables or volumes at runtime, keeping them out of source code.

questions.question: Which command creates a Cloud Storage bucket?
options: gcloud storage buckets create gs://BUCKET_NAME
gcloud create bucket BUCKET_NAME
gcloud storage new-bucket BUCKET_NAME
gsutil create gs://BUCKET_NAME
questions.explanation: The command 'gcloud storage buckets create gs://BUCKET_NAME' creates a new Cloud Storage bucket. You can specify location, storage class, and other settings. Bucket names must be globally unique across all Google Cloud customers.

questions.question: How can you ensure a Cloud Run service always has at least one warm instance to avoid cold start latency?
options: Set the minimum number of instances to 1 or more in the Cloud Run service configuration
Send a health check request every minute to keep an instance warm
Cloud Run always keeps one instance warm by default
Use Cloud Scheduler to invoke the service periodically
questions.explanation: Cloud Run allows you to configure a minimum number of instances. Setting min-instances to 1 or more ensures at least that many instances are always running, eliminating cold start latency for initial requests. Note that you pay for idle instances at a reduced rate.

questions.question: You need to deploy a stateful application on Compute Engine that stores data on a persistent disk. The application must survive zone failures. What should you do?
options: Use a regional persistent disk that replicates data across two zones, and create instances in both zones
Use a local SSD for faster performance
Create a snapshot every hour and restore in another zone if needed
Use a standard persistent disk and rely on automatic migration
questions.explanation: Regional persistent disks replicate data synchronously across two zones in a region. Combined with a managed instance group spanning those zones, this provides automatic failover with data persistence. Local SSDs do not survive instance termination. Hourly snapshots would mean up to an hour of data loss.

questions.question: What is the app.yaml file used for in App Engine?
options: It defines the App Engine application configuration including runtime, scaling settings, and handlers
It stores application secrets and credentials
It defines the database schema for the application
It configures the CI/CD pipeline
questions.explanation: The app.yaml file is the main configuration file for App Engine applications. It specifies the runtime (e.g., python311), scaling type (automatic, basic, or manual), instance class, URL handlers, environment variables, and other deployment settings.

questions.question: You need to set up a Pub/Sub topic and subscription for a message queue. Which commands create these resources?
options: gcloud pubsub topics create TOPIC_NAME, then gcloud pubsub subscriptions create SUB_NAME --topic=TOPIC_NAME
gcloud messaging create-topic TOPIC_NAME
gcloud queues create TOPIC_NAME
gcloud pubsub create TOPIC_NAME SUB_NAME
questions.explanation: Pub/Sub requires first creating a topic (the channel), then creating subscriptions (the consumers). The 'gcloud pubsub topics create' and 'gcloud pubsub subscriptions create --topic' commands set up this publish-subscribe pattern.

questions.question: You need a Cloud Function to process messages from a Pub/Sub topic but the processing occasionally takes longer than the retry timeout, causing duplicate processing. How should you address this?
options: Implement idempotent processing logic and use a Firestore document to track processed message IDs
Increase the function timeout to the maximum allowed
Disable retries on the function
Use a dead letter topic to catch unprocessed messages
questions.explanation: Idempotent processing ensures that processing the same message multiple times produces the same result. Tracking processed message IDs in Firestore (or another store) prevents duplicate processing. This is a best practice for Pub/Sub-triggered functions, as at-least-once delivery can produce duplicates.

questions.question: Which command copies files from a local machine to a Cloud Storage bucket?
options: gcloud storage cp LOCAL_FILE gs://BUCKET_NAME/
gcloud upload LOCAL_FILE gs://BUCKET_NAME/
gcloud storage move LOCAL_FILE gs://BUCKET_NAME/
gcloud copy LOCAL_FILE gs://BUCKET_NAME/
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud storage cp' command copies files to, from, or between Cloud Storage buckets and local file systems. Use the -r flag for recursive copying of directories. The gsutil cp command also works for backward compatibility.

questions.question: How do you perform a rolling update of a GKE Deployment without downtime?
options: Update the container image in the Deployment spec using kubectl set image or kubectl apply, and Kubernetes performs a rolling update by default
Delete all pods and recreate them with the new image
Create a new Deployment and delete the old one
Scale the Deployment to zero, update the image, then scale back up
questions.explanation: Kubernetes Deployments use a rolling update strategy by default. When you update the container image (via kubectl set image or kubectl apply), Kubernetes gradually replaces old pods with new ones, maintaining the specified number of available pods throughout the update.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service provides infrastructure and application monitoring with metrics, dashboards, and alerting?
options: Cloud Monitoring
Cloud Logging
Cloud Trace
Cloud Debugger
questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring collects metrics from Google Cloud services and applications, provides dashboards for visualization, and supports alerting policies that notify you when metrics exceed thresholds. It is part of Google Cloud's operations suite.

questions.question: Which service collects, stores, and allows querying of log data from Google Cloud resources?
options: Cloud Logging
Cloud Monitoring
BigQuery
Cloud Storage
questions.explanation: Cloud Logging automatically collects logs from Google Cloud services. You can also send custom application logs. It provides a log explorer for querying, filtering, and analyzing logs. Logs can be exported to Cloud Storage, BigQuery, or Pub/Sub for further processing.

questions.question: You want to be notified when CPU utilization on your Compute Engine VMs exceeds 80% for more than 5 minutes. What should you set up?
options: A Cloud Monitoring alerting policy with a CPU utilization metric condition and notification channel
A cron job on each VM that checks CPU and sends email
A Cloud Function that queries the Compute Engine API every minute
A Cloud Logging filter that matches CPU log entries
questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring alerting policies allow you to define conditions based on metrics (like CPU utilization), set duration thresholds (5 minutes), and configure notification channels (email, Slack, PagerDuty, Pub/Sub). This is the standard approach for infrastructure alerts.

questions.question: Which Cloud Logging feature allows you to route logs to specific destinations like BigQuery for long-term analysis?
options: Log sinks (log routers)
Log-based metrics
Log retention settings
Log exclusion filters
questions.explanation: Log sinks (also called log routers) allow you to route log entries to destinations like Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Pub/Sub, or another project's Cloud Logging. You can create filters to route only specific logs, enabling long-term analysis and compliance archival.

questions.question: You need to create a custom Cloud Monitoring metric that tracks the number of 500 errors in your application logs. What approach should you use?
options: Create a log-based metric in Cloud Logging that counts log entries matching a filter for 500 status codes
Write a custom application that polls Cloud Logging and pushes metrics to Cloud Monitoring
Export all logs to BigQuery and create a scheduled query
Use Cloud Trace to track error rates
questions.explanation: Log-based metrics in Cloud Logging allow you to create Cloud Monitoring metrics from log entries. You define a filter (e.g., httpRequest.status=500) and Cloud Logging automatically creates a metric that can be used in dashboards, alerting policies, and SLOs.

questions.question: Which command stops a running Compute Engine instance?
options: gcloud compute instances stop INSTANCE_NAME
gcloud compute instances shutdown INSTANCE_NAME
gcloud compute instances terminate INSTANCE_NAME
gcloud compute instances pause INSTANCE_NAME
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud compute instances stop' command gracefully stops a running VM. The VM's persistent disks are preserved, but you are not charged for the VM while it is stopped. You continue to pay for attached persistent disks and reserved static IPs.

questions.question: You need to resize a Compute Engine VM's machine type. What is the correct procedure?
options: Stop the VM, change the machine type, and start the VM again
Change the machine type while the VM is running via live migration
Delete the VM and create a new one with the desired machine type
Attach additional CPUs to the running VM
questions.explanation: To change a VM's machine type, you must first stop the VM, then modify the machine type using the Console or 'gcloud compute instances set-machine-type', and then start the VM. Machine type changes cannot be performed on running instances.

questions.question: What is the purpose of snapshot schedules for Compute Engine persistent disks?
options: To automatically create regular backups of persistent disks at defined intervals
To replicate disk data across regions in real time
To optimize disk performance by defragmenting data
To compress disk data and reduce storage costs
questions.explanation: Snapshot schedules automate the creation of persistent disk snapshots at regular intervals (hourly, daily, weekly). Snapshots are incremental and can be used to restore data or create new disks. They also support retention policies to automatically delete old snapshots.

questions.question: What is the purpose of managed instance group auto-scaling?
options: To automatically add or remove VM instances based on utilization metrics or schedules
To automatically update the operating system on VMs
To automatically migrate VMs to cheaper regions
To automatically resize disk storage on VMs
questions.explanation: Auto-scaling in managed instance groups automatically adjusts the number of VM instances based on metrics like CPU utilization, load balancer capacity, or custom metrics. You can also configure scheduled scaling for predictable traffic patterns.

questions.question: How do you scale a GKE node pool to have a minimum of 3 and maximum of 10 nodes?
options: Enable cluster autoscaler on the node pool with --min-nodes=3 and --max-nodes=10
Manually resize the node pool each time scaling is needed
Set resource quotas on the Kubernetes namespace
Configure horizontal pod autoscaler for the node pool
questions.explanation: GKE cluster autoscaler automatically adjusts the number of nodes in a node pool based on resource demands. Use 'gcloud container clusters update --enable-autoscaling --min-nodes=3 --max-nodes=10 --node-pool=POOL_NAME' to configure it.

questions.question: Your application experiences predictable traffic spikes every weekday at 9 AM. What scaling approach minimizes both cost and latency during the spike?
options: Configure scheduled scaling on the managed instance group to pre-scale instances before 9 AM
Set auto-scaling to a high minimum to always have enough instances
Manually add instances each morning before the spike
Use reactive auto-scaling based on CPU metrics only
questions.explanation: Scheduled scaling pre-creates instances before predicted traffic spikes, eliminating the delay of reactive auto-scaling. You set a schedule to increase capacity before 9 AM and reduce it after the spike. This is more cost-effective than maintaining a high minimum and faster than purely reactive scaling.

questions.question: A Compute Engine VM is unresponsive and you cannot SSH into it. What diagnostic steps should you take?
options: Check the serial port output via the Console, review Cloud Logging for boot and application logs, and check Cloud Monitoring for system metrics
Delete the VM and create a new one
Wait 24 hours for the VM to recover automatically
Contact Google Cloud support immediately
questions.explanation: Serial port output provides boot-level diagnostics even when SSH is unavailable. Cloud Logging captures system and application logs. Cloud Monitoring shows CPU, memory, disk, and network metrics. Together these provide comprehensive diagnostics for troubleshooting unresponsive VMs.

questions.question: What is the default retention period for Cloud Logging log entries?
options: 30 days for most logs, 400 days for Admin Activity audit logs
7 days for all logs
90 days for all logs
Logs are retained indefinitely
questions.explanation: Cloud Logging retains most log data for 30 days by default. Admin Activity audit logs are retained for 400 days. To retain logs longer, you must create log sinks to export them to Cloud Storage, BigQuery, or another destination.

questions.question: How do you SSH into a Compute Engine instance using the gcloud CLI?
options: gcloud compute ssh INSTANCE_NAME
gcloud ssh INSTANCE_NAME
gcloud connect INSTANCE_NAME
gcloud compute connect INSTANCE_NAME
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud compute ssh INSTANCE_NAME' command establishes an SSH connection to a Compute Engine instance. It automatically manages SSH keys by generating them and adding the public key to the instance's metadata if needed.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Cloud Run minimum instances configuration?
options: To keep a specified number of instances warm and avoid cold start latency
To set the total number of requests per instance
To limit the maximum memory each instance can use
To define the number of concurrent requests per instance
questions.explanation: Minimum instances in Cloud Run keeps the specified number of instances warm and ready to serve requests, eliminating cold start latency. Idle instances are billed at a reduced rate. This is useful for latency-sensitive applications that need immediate response times.

questions.question: You need to monitor a custom application metric (active users) that is not automatically collected by Google Cloud. How should you approach this?
options: Use the Cloud Monitoring API or OpenTelemetry to send custom metrics from your application
Create a log-based metric from application logs
Derive the metric from CPU utilization
Custom metrics are not supported in Cloud Monitoring
questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring supports custom metrics sent via the Monitoring API or OpenTelemetry. Your application can instrument code to record the 'active users' metric and send it to Cloud Monitoring. Custom metrics can then be used in dashboards, alerting policies, and auto-scaling decisions.

questions.question: What happens when you perform a rolling update on a managed instance group?
options: Instances are gradually replaced with new instances based on the updated template, maintaining a specified number of available instances during the update
All instances are stopped simultaneously and replaced
A completely new instance group is created and the old one is deleted
Only the configuration is updated; existing instances are not replaced
questions.explanation: Rolling updates in MIGs gradually replace instances with new ones based on the updated instance template. You can configure the maximum surge (extra instances during update) and maximum unavailable (instances that can be offline) to control the update pace and availability.

questions.question: Your GKE application needs to scale based on the number of messages in a Pub/Sub subscription, not just CPU or memory. How should you configure this?
options: Use a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) with a custom metric from Cloud Monitoring that tracks Pub/Sub subscription backlog
Manually scale pods based on monitoring dashboards
Use the cluster autoscaler with a Pub/Sub trigger
Set up a CronJob to check the subscription depth and adjust replicas
questions.explanation: The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) in GKE can scale based on custom metrics from Cloud Monitoring, including Pub/Sub metrics like subscription/num_undelivered_messages. You configure the Custom Metrics Adapter to expose Cloud Monitoring metrics to the HPA.

questions.question: Which command lists all running Compute Engine instances in the current project?
options: gcloud compute instances list
gcloud list instances
gcloud compute vms list
gcloud instances show-all
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud compute instances list' command displays all Compute Engine instances in the current project, including their name, zone, machine type, internal IP, external IP, and status.

questions.question: What is Cloud Trace used for?
options: Analyzing application latency by tracking requests across distributed services
Monitoring CPU and memory usage of VMs
Viewing application log entries
Debugging code in real time
questions.explanation: Cloud Trace is a distributed tracing system that collects latency data from applications. It shows how requests propagate through microservices, helping identify performance bottlenecks and latency issues. It integrates with App Engine, Cloud Run, GKE, and Compute Engine.

questions.question: You need to reduce costs for a fleet of development Compute Engine VMs that are only used during business hours (9 AM - 6 PM). What is the most effective approach?
options: Use instance schedules to automatically start VMs at 9 AM and stop them at 6 PM
Use Spot VMs for all development instances
Manually stop VMs at the end of each day
Use smaller machine types that run 24/7
questions.explanation: Instance schedules automatically start and stop VMs at defined times. For VMs needed only during business hours, this reduces costs by ~62% (running 9 hours instead of 24). This is more reliable than manual processes and avoids the preemption risk of Spot VMs for development work.

questions.question: How do you view the current number of replicas for a GKE Deployment?
options: kubectl get deployment DEPLOYMENT_NAME
gcloud container get-replicas DEPLOYMENT_NAME
kubectl show replicas DEPLOYMENT_NAME
gcloud gke describe DEPLOYMENT_NAME
questions.explanation: The 'kubectl get deployment DEPLOYMENT_NAME' command shows the desired and current number of replicas, along with ready and available counts. You can also use 'kubectl describe deployment' for more detailed information about the deployment state.

questions.question: You need to set up an uptime check that monitors your application's health endpoint and alerts if it is unreachable from multiple regions. Which service should you use?
options: Cloud Monitoring uptime checks with an alerting policy
Cloud Scheduler with a Cloud Function that pings the endpoint
A Compute Engine VM running a monitoring script
Cloud DNS health checks
questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring uptime checks periodically probe your application from multiple global locations. If the check fails from configured regions, it triggers an alerting policy that notifies you via email, SMS, PagerDuty, or other channels. This provides external, multi-region health monitoring.

questions.question: What is the recommended way to update the operating system on a managed instance group's VMs?
options: Create a new instance template with the updated image and perform a rolling update on the MIG
SSH into each VM and run apt-get upgrade
Use OS Config to push updates to all VMs simultaneously
Delete the MIG and recreate it with the new image
questions.explanation: The recommended approach is to create a new instance template with the updated OS image and perform a rolling update on the MIG. This ensures all VMs are based on the same image, maintains instance group consistency, and supports gradual rollout with rollback capability.

questions.question: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling in Google Cloud?
options: Horizontal scaling adds more instances while vertical scaling increases the resources (CPU/memory) of existing instances
Horizontal scaling increases disk size while vertical scaling adds more disks
They are the same thing with different names
Horizontal scaling is for databases while vertical scaling is for compute
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling (scaling out) adds more instances to distribute load. Vertical scaling (scaling up) increases the CPU, memory, or other resources of existing instances. Horizontal scaling provides better availability and fault tolerance, while vertical scaling is simpler but has hardware limits.

questions.question: Which tool provides a visual dashboard for viewing Cloud Logging data?
options: Logs Explorer
Cloud Shell
Cloud Console Home
Resource Manager
questions.explanation: The Logs Explorer in the Google Cloud Console provides a visual interface for querying, viewing, and analyzing log data. It supports filters, saved queries, histograms, and the ability to view logs in real time.

questions.question: A GKE pod is crashing with an OOMKilled status. What does this indicate and how should you resolve it?
options: The pod exceeded its memory limit; increase the memory resource request and limit in the pod specification
The pod has a syntax error in its code; check the application logs
The node ran out of disk space; add more storage to the node pool
The container image is corrupted; rebuild and push a new image
questions.explanation: OOMKilled means the container exceeded its memory limit and was terminated by Kubernetes. The fix is to increase the memory resources in the pod spec (requests and limits). Analyze the application's memory usage patterns to set appropriate values. Also check for memory leaks in the application code.

questions.question: How do you configure a managed instance group to automatically replace unhealthy VMs?
options: Configure a health check on the managed instance group that defines healthy/unhealthy criteria
Auto-healing is always enabled by default with no configuration needed
Set up a Cloud Function that monitors instances and replaces them
Create a Cloud Scheduler job to restart unhealthy VMs
questions.explanation: MIG auto-healing uses health checks to determine if instances are healthy. You configure an HTTP, HTTPS, or TCP health check with the MIG. If an instance fails the health check for the specified duration, the MIG automatically recreates it from the instance template.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Cloud Monitoring notification channels?
options: To define where alerting policy notifications are sent, such as email, SMS, Slack, or PagerDuty
To route network traffic based on monitoring data
To send monitoring metrics to external systems
To define who can view monitoring dashboards
questions.explanation: Notification channels in Cloud Monitoring define the delivery endpoints for alerting policy notifications. Supported channels include email, SMS, Slack, PagerDuty, webhooks, and mobile push notifications. Multiple channels can be configured per alerting policy.

questions.question: You need to transfer a large dataset (100 TB) from an on-premises data center to Cloud Storage. Network transfer would take weeks. What is the fastest option?
options: Use Transfer Appliance, a physical device shipped to your location for offline data transfer
Use gsutil rsync to transfer data over the internet
Set up a Dedicated Interconnect and transfer over the private connection
Compress the data and transfer via Cloud VPN
questions.explanation: Transfer Appliance is a physical, high-capacity storage server that is shipped to your data center. You copy the data locally, ship it back to Google, and the data is uploaded to Cloud Storage. For 100 TB, this is significantly faster than network transfer methods.

questions.question: What are the three types of IAM roles in Google Cloud?
options: Basic (primitive), predefined, and custom roles
Admin, editor, and viewer roles
Project, folder, and organization roles
Read, write, and execute roles
questions.explanation: Google Cloud IAM has three types of roles: Basic roles (Owner, Editor, Viewer - formerly called primitive roles), Predefined roles (granular roles created by Google for specific services), and Custom roles (user-defined roles with specific permissions).

questions.question: Which IAM role grants the most permissions and should be avoided in production?
options: roles/owner (Basic Owner)
roles/editor (Basic Editor)
roles/viewer (Basic Viewer)
roles/iam.admin
questions.explanation: The Owner role grants full control over all resources in a project, including the ability to manage IAM policies and billing. It should be avoided in production and replaced with more granular predefined or custom roles that follow the principle of least privilege.

questions.question: You need to grant a developer the ability to manage Compute Engine instances but not modify IAM policies or billing. Which role should you assign?
options: roles/compute.admin
roles/owner
roles/editor
roles/viewer
questions.explanation: The Compute Admin role (roles/compute.admin) grants full control over Compute Engine resources without access to IAM policies or billing. This follows the principle of least privilege by granting only the permissions needed for the developer's job function.

questions.question: What is the principle of least privilege as it applies to Google Cloud IAM?
options: Grant only the minimum permissions necessary for a user to perform their job function
Grant all users the Viewer role by default
Use only custom roles for all users
Grant permissions at the organization level so they apply everywhere
questions.explanation: The principle of least privilege means granting only the permissions necessary for a user to perform their specific tasks. In Google Cloud, this means using predefined or custom roles instead of basic roles and granting permissions at the most specific resource level possible.

questions.question: What is a service account in Google Cloud?
options: A special identity used by applications and VMs to authenticate to Google Cloud APIs
A personal email account used for billing
A group of users with shared permissions
An account used only for the Google Cloud Console
questions.explanation: A service account is a special type of account used by applications, VMs, and services (not humans) to authenticate with Google Cloud APIs. Service accounts can be granted IAM roles and use keys or workload identity for authentication.

questions.question: Why should you avoid creating and downloading service account keys when possible?
options: Service account keys are long-lived credentials that pose a security risk if compromised; workload identity federation is preferred
Service account keys expire after 24 hours
Keys can only be used with one API at a time
Google Cloud does not support service account keys
questions.explanation: Service account keys are long-lived credentials that can be compromised through exposure, theft, or accidental commits. Workload Identity Federation, attached service accounts, and other keyless authentication methods are more secure because they use short-lived, automatically managed credentials.

questions.question: A Compute Engine VM needs to access Cloud Storage buckets. What is the most secure way to grant this access?
options: Attach a service account to the VM with the appropriate Cloud Storage IAM role
Create a service account key and store it on the VM's disk
Grant the VM's default service account the Editor role
Make the Cloud Storage bucket publicly accessible
questions.explanation: Attaching a service account to a Compute Engine VM is the most secure approach. The VM automatically obtains short-lived credentials from the metadata server without any keys to manage. Grant the service account only the specific Cloud Storage role needed (e.g., Storage Object Viewer).

questions.question: Where can you view Cloud Audit Logs in Google Cloud?
options: In Cloud Logging using the Logs Explorer
Only in the Security Command Center
Only by exporting to BigQuery
In the Billing Console
questions.explanation: Cloud Audit Logs are part of Cloud Logging and can be viewed in the Logs Explorer. There are four types of audit logs: Admin Activity, Data Access, System Event, and Policy Denied. Admin Activity logs are always enabled and retained for 400 days.

questions.question: Which type of Cloud Audit Log records when a user reads resource data, such as listing objects in a Cloud Storage bucket?
options: Data Access audit logs
Admin Activity audit logs
System Event audit logs
Policy Denied audit logs
questions.explanation: Data Access audit logs record API calls that read resource configurations or user-provided data (e.g., listing, getting, or reading resources). They are disabled by default (except for BigQuery) because they can generate large volumes of log data.

questions.question: Your security team needs to audit all IAM policy changes across the entire organization. Which audit log type captures this information and at what level should you configure the log sink?
options: Admin Activity audit logs captured by an aggregated log sink at the organization level
Data Access audit logs at the project level
System Event logs at the folder level
Policy Denied logs at the billing account level
questions.explanation: IAM policy changes are recorded in Admin Activity audit logs, which are always enabled. An aggregated log sink at the organization level captures these events from all projects and folders. The sink can export to BigQuery for long-term analysis and compliance reporting.

questions.question: What is a VPC firewall rule used for?
options: To control network traffic to and from VM instances in a VPC
To encrypt data stored in Cloud Storage
To manage IAM permissions for users
To route DNS queries
questions.explanation: VPC firewall rules control incoming (ingress) and outgoing (egress) network traffic for VM instances. Rules are defined by direction, priority, source/destination, protocol/port, and action (allow/deny). They are applied at the instance level using network tags or service accounts.

questions.question: Which command creates a firewall rule that allows SSH traffic (port 22) to instances with the tag 'web-server'?
options: gcloud compute firewall-rules create allow-ssh --allow tcp:22 --target-tags=web-server
gcloud firewall create allow-ssh --port=22 --tag=web-server
gcloud network firewall allow-ssh --tcp=22 --targets=web-server
gcloud compute create-firewall allow-ssh --allow=ssh --tag=web-server
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud compute firewall-rules create' command creates a VPC firewall rule. The --allow flag specifies the protocol and port (tcp:22 for SSH), and --target-tags restricts the rule to only instances with the specified tag.

questions.question: Your VPC has a default deny-all ingress rule. You need to allow HTTP traffic only from a specific IP range to instances tagged 'web'. You also need to deny traffic from a known malicious IP within that range. What firewall configuration achieves this?
