options: Create an allow rule for the IP range at priority 1000 and a deny rule for the malicious IP at a lower priority number (higher priority)
Create a single allow rule that excludes the malicious IP
Create a deny rule for the malicious IP and rely on implicit allow
Use Cloud Armor instead of firewall rules
questions.explanation: Firewall rules are evaluated by priority (lower number = higher priority). Create a deny rule for the malicious IP at a lower priority number (e.g., 900) and an allow rule for the broader range at a higher number (e.g., 1000). The deny rule takes effect first for the malicious IP.

questions.question: What is Private Google Access and when should you enable it?
options: It allows VM instances without external IP addresses to access Google APIs and services through internal IP addresses
It restricts Google employees from accessing your data
It provides private DNS resolution for Google services
It encrypts all traffic between your VPC and Google services
questions.explanation: Private Google Access allows VMs with only internal IP addresses (no external IPs) to reach Google APIs and services like Cloud Storage, BigQuery, and others. It is enabled at the subnet level and is essential for private VMs that need to access Google services.

questions.question: You need to create a custom IAM role that combines permissions from two different predefined roles. Which command creates the custom role?
options: gcloud iam roles create ROLE_ID --project=PROJECT_ID --permissions=PERMISSIONS
gcloud iam custom-roles create ROLE_ID
gcloud roles create ROLE_ID --combine=ROLE1,ROLE2
Custom roles can only be created in the Console
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud iam roles create' command creates a custom IAM role with specified permissions. You provide the role ID, project or organization, and a comma-separated list of permissions. You can also use a YAML file with --file to define the role.

questions.question: What is a default service account and why should you be cautious about using it?
options: It is automatically created for each project with the Editor role, which grants overly broad permissions
It is a service account without any permissions
It is a Google-managed account that cannot be modified
It is the account used by the Cloud Console
questions.explanation: Default service accounts (like the Compute Engine default service account) are automatically created and granted the Editor role, which is overly permissive. Best practice is to create dedicated service accounts with minimal permissions for each application instead of using the default account.

questions.question: Which command grants an IAM role to a user on a specific project?
options: gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --member=user:EMAIL --role=ROLE
gcloud iam grant ROLE to USER on PROJECT_ID
gcloud projects grant-role PROJECT_ID USER ROLE
gcloud iam add-binding PROJECT_ID USER ROLE
questions.explanation: The 'gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding' command adds an IAM policy binding to a project. You specify the member (user:, serviceAccount:, or group:) and the role. The same pattern works for organizations, folders, and individual resources.

questions.question: Which audit log type is always enabled and cannot be disabled?
options: Admin Activity audit logs
Data Access audit logs
System Event audit logs
Policy Denied audit logs
questions.explanation: Admin Activity audit logs record administrative actions (creating, deleting, or modifying resources and IAM policies). They are always enabled and cannot be disabled or configured. They are retained for 400 days at no charge.

questions.question: What is the default behavior of VPC firewall rules for incoming traffic?
options: All incoming traffic is denied by default (implied deny-all ingress rule)
All incoming traffic is allowed by default
Only HTTP and HTTPS traffic is allowed by default
Only traffic from within the VPC is allowed by default
questions.explanation: VPC networks have an implied deny-all ingress rule that blocks all incoming traffic by default. You must create explicit allow rules for traffic you want to permit. There is also an implied allow-all egress rule that permits all outgoing traffic by default.

questions.question: A Cloud Function needs to access a Cloud SQL database in another project. What is the recommended approach for authentication?
options: Create a service account in the database project, grant it Cloud SQL Client role, and configure the Cloud Function to use that service account
Use the Cloud Function's default service account and grant it Owner role on the database project
Share the Cloud SQL admin password with the Cloud Function via environment variables
Make the Cloud SQL database publicly accessible
questions.explanation: Create a dedicated service account with only the Cloud SQL Client role for cross-project access. The Cloud Function can impersonate this service account or use it directly. This follows least privilege and avoids sharing credentials or over-permissioning.

questions.question: How does IAM policy inheritance work in the Google Cloud resource hierarchy?
options: Policies are inherited from parent resources and combined with policies at the current level; more permissive policies always win
Policies at lower levels override policies from higher levels
Policies do not inherit; each level must have its own complete policy
Only organization-level policies are inherited
questions.explanation: IAM policies are inherited downward through the resource hierarchy (Organization > Folder > Project > Resource). Policies are additive, meaning a role granted at a higher level applies to all resources below. You cannot restrict inherited access at a lower level; IAM is always additive (allow-only).

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service provides DDoS protection and WAF capabilities for web applications?
options: Cloud Armor
Cloud Firewall
Cloud IDS
VPC Flow Logs
questions.explanation: Cloud Armor provides DDoS protection and WAF (Web Application Firewall) capabilities. It integrates with the global load balancer to filter traffic based on IP addresses, geographic regions, and request attributes. It includes preconfigured rules for OWASP Top 10 threats.

questions.question: What is Workload Identity in GKE?
options: A feature that allows GKE pods to use IAM service accounts without managing keys
A networking feature for pod-to-pod communication
A container image scanning tool
A GKE node identity verification system
questions.explanation: Workload Identity is the recommended way for GKE workloads to access Google Cloud APIs. It maps Kubernetes service accounts to IAM service accounts, allowing pods to authenticate without service account keys. This provides fine-grained, per-pod identity management.

questions.question: You need to detect and alert on any attempts to access resources outside normal business hours. Which combination of services should you use?
options: Enable Data Access audit logs and create a log-based metric with a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy filtered by timestamp
Use VPC Flow Logs to track access times
Set up Cloud Scheduler to disable all IAM roles after business hours
Configure IAM Conditions to deny access outside business hours
questions.explanation: Data Access audit logs capture resource access events with timestamps. A log-based metric filtered for access outside business hours can trigger a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy. While IAM Conditions can restrict access by time, the question asks about detection and alerting, not prevention.

questions.question: You need to ensure that VMs in your VPC cannot access the public internet but can still access Google Cloud APIs. What configuration is required?
options: Remove external IPs from VMs, enable Private Google Access on the subnets, and do not create a Cloud NAT
Create a deny-all egress firewall rule
Use VPC Service Controls to block internet access
Disable the Compute Engine API
questions.explanation: Removing external IPs prevents direct internet access. Enabling Private Google Access allows VMs to reach Google APIs through internal IP addresses. Without Cloud NAT, VMs cannot reach non-Google internet destinations. This provides a secure configuration for internal workloads.

questions.question: What is an Organization Policy in Google Cloud?
options: A constraint applied to the resource hierarchy that restricts certain configurations across the organization
An IAM role that grants organization-wide access
A billing policy that limits spending across projects
A network policy that controls traffic between organizations
questions.explanation: Organization Policies define constraints on resources. They can restrict actions like which regions resources can be created in, whether external IP addresses are allowed, or which APIs can be enabled. Policies are inherited through the resource hierarchy.

questions.question: An application running on an on-premises server needs to access Google Cloud resources. The security team prohibits exporting service account keys. What should you use?
options: Workload Identity Federation to exchange the on-premises identity provider's tokens for Google Cloud access tokens
Create a VPN tunnel and use the VM's metadata server
Grant the on-premises server IP address direct API access
There is no way to access Google Cloud without service account keys from on-premises
questions.explanation: Workload Identity Federation allows external workloads to access Google Cloud resources without service account keys. It exchanges tokens from external identity providers (OIDC, SAML, AWS) for short-lived Google Cloud access tokens. This eliminates the security risk of managing long-lived keys.

questions.question: What information is captured in Admin Activity audit logs?
options: API calls that modify the configuration or metadata of resources, such as creating, updating, or deleting resources
All data read operations by users
Network traffic flow data between VMs
CPU and memory utilization of compute resources
questions.explanation: Admin Activity audit logs capture API calls that modify resource configuration or metadata. Examples include creating a VM, changing IAM policies, modifying a Cloud SQL instance, and deleting a Cloud Storage bucket. These logs help track who made changes and when.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud feature allows VMs without external IP addresses to access the internet for software updates?
options: Cloud NAT
Private Google Access
Cloud VPN
Cloud CDN
questions.explanation: Cloud NAT (Network Address Translation) allows VMs without external IP addresses to access the internet for outgoing connections (like software updates). It provides source NAT for VMs in the VPC without exposing them to incoming internet traffic. Unlike Private Google Access, it works for all internet destinations.

questions.question: You need to grant a contractor temporary access to a specific Cloud Storage bucket for 30 days. What is the best approach using IAM?
options: Use an IAM Condition with a time-based expression that automatically expires the role binding after 30 days
Grant the role and set a calendar reminder to remove it manually
Create a temporary project with the bucket and delete the project after 30 days
Grant access and create a Cloud Function that removes the binding after 30 days
questions.explanation: IAM Conditions support time-based expressions that automatically expire access. You can set a condition like 'request.time < timestamp("2026-05-01T00:00:00Z")' on the IAM policy binding. This eliminates the risk of forgotten access and manual cleanup.
